serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish portion of Bombay Aloo Masala is approximately 300 g, typically served alongside rice, naan, or roti as part of an Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; USDA FoodData Central — Potatoes, boiled/fried, and spiced curry dishes
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; U.S. Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for Indian potato curry dishes and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for deep-fried potatoes and spiced vegetable gravies. The input values (energy 150 kcal, protein 2.67 g, fat 8.33 g, carbohydrate 16.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges for aloo masala preparations and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 250 mg/100 g is plausible for a restaurant preparation. Micronutrient values (calcium 16.67 mg, iron 0.67 mg, vitamin C 8.33 mg) are consistent with ICMR-NIN potato curry data and are retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Potato curry analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bombay Aloo Masala (Spicy). GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled/fried potato curry (GI ~65, medium category), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables for potato-based curry dishes. Deep-frying raises the GI relative to boiled potato (GI ~50–60) due to AGE formation and altered starch structure; the spiced gravy with onion, tomato, and fibre moderates the response slightly. GL per serving = (65 × 16.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving) / 100 = 31.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 250 mg/100 g; 750 mg per 300 g serving — approaching 33% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single side dish
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 150 g, as a full 300 g serving delivers 750 mg sodium; the potassium content from potatoes, tomatoes, and onions (~380 mg/100 g estimated) provides a partial DASH-aligned offset but does not fully neutralise the sodium load at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~31 per 300 g serving) from deep-fried high-GI potatoes with only 2.0 g/100 g dietary fibre insufficient to blunt postprandial glucose spike
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to a glycemic load of approximately 31 per standard serving; if consumed, portion should be reduced to 100–150 g, paired with a high-fibre food such as dal or raita, and preparation should be requested as baked or boiled potato rather than deep-fried to reduce GI and AGE formation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 8.33 g/100 g (24.99 g per 300 g serving) from deep-frying oil; saturated fat 1.33 g/100 g; trans fat 0.07 g/100 g; cholesterol 0 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high total fat content from deep-frying (approximately 25 g fat per 300 g serving) and the presence of trans fat (0.07 g/100 g); requesting pan-fried or baked potato preparation and reducing portion size to 150 g would meaningfully lower the atherogenic fat load while retaining the cardioprotective benefits of turmeric, garlic, and ginger in the spice blend.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Multiple nightshade ingredients (potato, tomato, green chilli) combined with deep-frying AGE generation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to the combination of nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, green chilli — all containing solanine or capsaicin) and deep-frying which generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; the anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) are beneficial but insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory preparation method and ingredient profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bombay Aloo Masala (Spicy) is a classic North Indian potato curry dish in which potato cubes are first deep-fried until golden and crisp, then simmered in a richly spiced gravy made from sautéed onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, and garam masala. The dish originates from the street-food and home-cooking traditions of Mumbai (Bombay) and is widely served across Indian restaurants in Singapore as a vegetarian side dish alongside rice, naan, or roti. The texture contrasts crispy-edged potato with a thick, tangy, and moderately spicy tomato-onion gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 150 kcal, 2.67 g protein, 8.33 g fat (1.33 g saturated), 16.0 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 250 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) with a glycemic load of approximately 31 per 300 g serving, making it a high-GL dish unsuitable for uncontrolled Type 2 Diabetes. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. Anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric (curcumin), ginger, and garlic provide functional benefits. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free. It is commonly served as a side dish at lunch or dinner in North Indian restaurants across Singapore.

No exact published nutrient profile for Bombay Aloo Masala (Spicy) as served by this specific Singapore restaurant was found in an approved primary source. The input values are consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges and are retained with medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for potato curry dishes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific restaurant preparation of Bombay Aloo Masala are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero. trans_fat_g is 0.07 g/100 g as reported in the input, consistent with trace trans fats from deep-frying oil; this is a plausible trace value and is retained. The dish is served at Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine, South Bridge Road, Singapore (GrabFood listing). Primary cuisine context is North Indian; country of origin for this serving context is Singapore. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Bombay Aloo Masala (Spicy) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. GL per 300 g serving = (65 × 16.0 × 300) / 100 / 100 = 31.2 g.
